subject: [RFC PATCH v2 0/4] mm/zsmalloc: reduce zs_free() latency on swap release path

reviews.author: Nhat Pham (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that free_zspage() and kmem_cache_free() are not the main contributors to zs_free latency, but expressed uncertainty about optimizing free_zspage(), suggesting deferring might be the only option.
reviews.raw_body:
Ah OK - I found this thread now:

https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260414054930.225853-1-xueyuan.chen21@gmail.com/

Hmm, free_zspage() and kmem_cache_free().

* kmem_cache_free() is just handle freeing. Bulk-freeing?

* free_zspage() looks like just ordinary teardown work :( Seems like
we're not spinning any lock here - we just try lock the backing pages,
and the rest is normal work. Not sure how to optimize this - perhaps
deferring is the only way.

2026-04-21.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function zs_free_deferred() for asynchronous handle freeing in zsmalloc. The original implementation of locking, grabbing a handle, and unlocking one at a time is inefficient and can be improved by using a workqueue or per-cpu-fying the batches/queues to reduce concurrency issues. An alternative approach suggested is to use two arrays of handles, swapping them under a lock when the defer worker is woken up, and then freeing the handles in the old array.

reviews.author: Xueyuan Chen
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Xueyuan Chen noted that the current implementation of zsmalloc free operations may not be optimized for contiguous ranges of swap slots to be freed, suggesting batching opportunities to reduce overhead.

The reviewer noted that free_zspage() acquires the class->lock while holding the zone->lock, creating a nested lock dependency and significantly extending the hold time of the class->lock, which causes other CPUs to wait longer.

2026-04-22.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function zs_free_deferred() for asynchronous handle freeing in zsmalloc. The original implementation of locking, grabbing a handle, and unlocking one at a time is inefficient and can be improved by using a workqueue or per-cpu-fying the batches/queues to reduce concurrency issues. An alternative approach suggested is to use two arrays of handles, swapping them under a lock when the defer worker is woken up, and then freeing the handles in the old array.
